title: Graph algorithms: spanning trees, Chinese postman and travelling salesman
summary: Three classic network problems, one lesson. A minimum spanning tree connects every vertex as cheaply as possible; you build it with Kruskal's algorithm (sort the edges, add the cheapest that makes no cycle) or with Prim's algorithm (grow one tree outward from a starting vertex). The Chinese postman (route inspection) problem asks for the shortest closed walk that covers every edge: you pair up the odd-degree vertices and repeat the cheapest pairing. The travelling salesman problem asks for the shortest closed tour that visits every vertex once; because it is intractable to solve exactly, you trap the optimum between an upper bound from the nearest-neighbour algorithm and a lower bound from the deleted-vertex minimum spanning tree. This is Higher Level content, and every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks.
learningObjectives: Build a minimum spanning tree with Kruskal's algorithm and with Prim's algorithm, and state its total weight.
Solve the Chinese postman (route inspection) problem by pairing odd-degree vertices and repeating the cheapest pairing.
Find an upper bound for the travelling salesman problem using the nearest-neighbour algorithm.
Find a lower bound for the travelling salesman problem by deleting a vertex, taking the minimum spanning tree of the rest and adding the two shortest deleted edges.
Choose the correct algorithm for a given situation and present working so that method marks and follow-through protect the final answer.

simpleExplanation.title: Networks Done Three Ways
simpleExplanation.summary: A weighted graph is a set of places (vertices) joined by routes (edges) that each carry a cost. This topic answers three different network questions with three different algorithms: connect everything cheaply, cover every road, or visit every place. The trick is spotting which question you are being asked, then following the matching recipe carefully.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a small town on a map. If a broadband company wants to lay cable so every house is connected using the least cable, it wants a minimum spanning tree — the skeleton that joins everyone with no wasteful loops. If a gritting lorry must drive down every street and return to the depot, that is the Chinese postman problem — cover every edge, and repeat as few streets as you can. If a delivery driver must call at every house once and come home, that is the travelling salesman problem — visit every vertex, shortest loop wins. Same map, three jobs, three methods.
steps: Read the question and classify it. Connect all points with no loops means spanning tree; cover all roads means Chinese postman; visit all places once means travelling salesman.
Gather the graph data. Note the weight on each edge and, for the postman, the degree (number of edges) at each vertex.
Run the matching algorithm carefully, keeping ordered working: Kruskal's or Prim's for the tree, odd-vertex pairing for the postman, nearest-neighbour and deleted-vertex bounds for the salesman.
State the answer in context with its total weight, and for the travelling salesman quote the interval lower bound to upper bound.

flashcards.back: A subgraph that connects all $v$ vertices of a graph using exactly $v-1$ edges and contains no cycle. Every vertex is reachable, and there are no wasteful loops.
Among all spanning trees of a weighted connected graph, the one whose edge weights add to the smallest total. It is the cheapest way to connect every vertex.
Sort all edges in increasing weight. Repeatedly add the cheapest remaining edge, but reject any edge that would form a cycle with those already chosen. Stop when $v-1$ edges have been selected.

Start from any one vertex. Repeatedly add the cheapest edge that joins a vertex already in the tree to a vertex not yet in the tree. Stop when all $v$ vertices are connected. It never needs a separate cycle check.

Both give an MST of the same total weight. Kruskal grows the cheapest edges anywhere and rejects cycles; Prim grows a single connected tree outward from a start vertex, so a partly built Prim tree is always connected while a partly built Kruskal forest may not be.
The number of edge-ends meeting at that vertex. The sum of all degrees is twice the number of edges, so the number of odd-degree vertices is always even.
A closed walk that uses every edge exactly once. It exists if and only if the graph is connected and every vertex has even degree; then the shortest postman route equals the sum of all edge weights.

Find the shortest closed walk that traverses every edge at least once. If some vertices have odd degree, some edges must be repeated; you choose which by pairing the odd-degree vertices as cheaply as possible.

List the odd-degree vertices. For each way of pairing them up, add the shortest-path distances of the pairs. Pick the pairing with the smallest total. Postman route = (sum of all edge weights) + (that minimum pairing total).
Find the shortest closed tour that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the start — a minimum-weight Hamiltonian cycle. There is no efficient exact method, so at this level we find bounds.
Start at a vertex, repeatedly travel to the nearest unvisited vertex, then close the tour by returning to the start. The total is one possible tour, so it is an upper bound for the optimum.
Delete one vertex and all its edges; find the MST of what remains; add the two shortest edges that were attached to the deleted vertex. The sum is a lower bound for the optimal tour.
The postman must cover every EDGE (roads); the salesman must visit every VERTEX (places) once. Mnemonic: Postman → Paths, Salesman → Stops.

For the TSP the optimal tour length $T$ satisfies (lower bound) $\le T \le$ (upper bound). A different starting vertex or a different deleted vertex changes the bound, so the best bounds are the largest lower bound and the smallest upper bound you can find.

quickCheck.prompt: A connected graph has 6 vertices. A minimum spanning tree of it must contain:
options: as many edges as possible without repeating a vertex

quickCheck.prompt: At each step, Kruskal's algorithm adds:
options: the cheapest remaining edge that does not create a cycle
the most expensive edge that avoids a cycle
any edge joining the current tree to a new vertex, chosen at random
quickCheck.answer: the cheapest remaining edge that does not create a cycle

quickCheck.prompt: Running the nearest-neighbour algorithm and summing the resulting tour gives you:
options: an upper bound for the travelling salesman problem
a lower bound for the travelling salesman problem
quickCheck.answer: an upper bound for the travelling salesman problem

quickCheck.prompt: To find a lower bound for the travelling salesman problem you should:
options: delete a vertex, find the MST of the rest, then add the two shortest deleted edges
find the MST of the whole graph and double it
delete a vertex, find the MST of the rest, then add the two longest deleted edges
run nearest neighbour from every vertex and take the smallest
quickCheck.answer: delete a vertex, find the MST of the rest, then add the two shortest deleted edges

quickCheck.prompt: A graph has exactly two odd-degree vertices, $P$ and $Q$, and its edges total 40. The shortest path from $P$ to $Q$ is 6. The shortest Chinese postman route has length:

faq.q: Do I have to know both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms?
faq.a: Yes. For AI HL you should be able to build a minimum spanning tree either way, and they always give the same total weight. Kruskal's works from a sorted list of edges and rejects any that would close a loop; Prim's grows one connected tree outward from a chosen vertex and so never needs a separate cycle test. Prim's is handy when the graph is given as a distance matrix, because you just repeatedly pick the smallest entry in the columns of vertices already reached.

faq.q: In the Chinese postman problem, what if there are four odd vertices?
faq.a: The number of odd-degree vertices is always even. With four odd vertices, say A, B, C, D, there are exactly three ways to split them into two pairs: (AB and CD), (AC and BD), (AD and BC). For each pairing add the two shortest-path distances, then pick the pairing with the smallest total. You add that total to the sum of all the edge weights. With six odd vertices there are 15 pairings, which is why the exam keeps these to two or four odd vertices.

faq.q: Why does the nearest-neighbour tour give an upper bound and not the answer?
faq.a: Nearest neighbour is greedy: it always jumps to the closest unvisited vertex without looking ahead. That short-sightedness can leave a very long edge to close the loop at the end, so the tour it produces is a valid tour but usually not the shortest one. Because it is a real tour, its length is at least the optimum, which makes it an upper bound. Trying every starting vertex and keeping the smallest gives the best (lowest) upper bound.

faq.q: Why does deleting a vertex give a lower bound for the TSP?
faq.a: Any Hamiltonian tour, if you remove one vertex and its two tour edges, leaves a path through the other vertices, and a path is a spanning tree of them, so it cannot weigh less than their MST. Adding back the two cheapest edges at the deleted vertex is the least the two removed edges could have cost. So MST-of-the-rest plus the two shortest deleted edges can never exceed the true tour: it is a lower bound. Deleting different vertices gives different lower bounds; the largest is the strongest.

faq.q: How do I tell which algorithm a question wants?
faq.a: Look at the goal. 'Connect every location using the least cable / cost / length, with no loops' is a minimum spanning tree. 'Travel along every road / street / pipe and return to the start' is the Chinese postman. 'Visit every town / customer / site once and return' is the travelling salesman. Covering edges is the postman; visiting vertices is the salesman; joining vertices with no repetition is the spanning tree.

sections.content: Logistics, telecoms and circuit design all reduce to the same picture: places joined by routes that carry a cost. This lesson works through the three network questions the AI HL course asks about such graphs. First, connect every vertex as cheaply as possible with a minimum spanning tree, built either by Kruskal's algorithm or by Prim's. Second, cover every edge and return home for the least distance with the Chinese postman problem. Third, visit every vertex once and return with the travelling salesman problem, which we cannot solve exactly but can trap between a lower and an upper bound. This is Higher Level material. As in every calculation topic, the worked examples below are laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice, so you can see where each method mark and accuracy mark is earned.
Minimum spanning trees: Kruskal's algorithm

Suppose you must connect several locations with cable, pipe or road, and you want every location joined using the least total length, with no wasteful loops. The solution is a minimum spanning tree (MST): a subgraph that reaches all $v$ vertices, uses exactly $v-1$ edges and contains no cycle, chosen so the sum of its edge weights is as small as possible. Kruskal's algorithm builds it by being greedy about individual edges.

items: **Goal:** connect all vertices with the smallest total edge weight, using $v-1$ edges and no cycle.
**Step 1:** list every edge in increasing order of weight.
**Step 2:** add the cheapest edge.
**Step 3:** keep adding the next-cheapest edge, but **reject** any edge that would create a cycle with edges already chosen.
**Step 4:** stop once $v-1$ edges are in the tree. Their total weight is the weight of the MST.

sections.question: A weighted graph on vertices A–E has edges AB 4, AC 3, BC 2, BD 5, CD 6, DE 4, CE 7. Use Kruskal's algorithm to find the minimum spanning tree and its total weight. [5]
sections.solution:
There are $v = 5$ vertices, so a spanning tree needs $v - 1 = 4$ edges.

**Step 1: sort the edges by weight.**
BC (2), AC (3), AB (4), DE (4), BD (5), CD (6), CE (7).

**Step 2: add edges cheapest first, rejecting any that make a cycle.**

| Edge | Weight | Decision | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| BC | 2 | Accept | connects B and C |
| AC | 3 | Accept | connects A |
| AB | 4 | Reject | would close cycle A–B–C–A |
| DE | 4 | Accept | connects D and E |
| BD | 5 | Accept | connects the D–E part to the rest |

After accepting BC, AC, DE, BD we have 4 edges joining all five vertices, so we stop. (CD and CE are never needed.)

**Minimum spanning tree:** edges BC, AC, BD, DE.

**Total weight:** $2 + 3 + 5 + 4 = 14.$

sections.content: Below is exactly how that 5-mark question is scored. The marks are analytic: a method mark (M) rewards a correct process, and an accuracy mark (A) rewards a correct value and depends on the method mark it follows. Follow-through (FT) means a slip early on need not cost the marks that build on it, provided the later step is correct on your own figures.

**How our marking engine awards the marks:**

- **M1 — order the edges.** Awarded for listing the edges in increasing weight, or for clearly starting from the smallest and working up: BC (2), AC (3), AB (4), DE (4), BD (5), ... The engine rewards the sorting process even if one weight is later miscopied.
- **M1 — add avoiding cycles.** Awarded for the greedy process: accept the cheapest edge, then keep adding the next cheapest while rejecting any that closes a cycle. Explicitly rejecting AB (and CD, CE) as cycle-forming earns this method mark.
- **A1 — correct MST edges.** Awarded for the correct set BC, AC, BD, DE. This accuracy mark stands on the two method marks above.
- **M1 — right number of edges to connect all vertices.** Awarded for stopping at $v - 1 = 4$ edges that connect all five vertices — neither leaving a vertex out nor adding a redundant fifth edge.
- **A1 — total 14.** Awarded for $2 + 3 + 5 + 4 = 14$. This is FT on the candidate's own tree: a student who selected a slightly different valid-looking set but summed their own edges correctly still earns this accuracy mark.

**Accept equivalent forms.** The engine accepts the tree listed in any order and accepts the total written as $2+3+4+5$ or $14$. Once the correct tree and total appear, ISW (ignore subsequent working) means a later restatement cannot lose the marks.

The Practice button marks YOUR working this same way — method mark by method mark, with dependent accuracy marks and follow-through. So always show the process: write the edges in weight order, show each accept/reject decision (naming the cycle you avoid), count to $v-1$ edges, then add. A bare 'MST = 14' with no sorted list and no rejections risks losing the two method marks even when the number is right; the shown method is what protects your score if a weight slips.
The same tree, Prim's way

Prim's algorithm reaches the identical minimum spanning tree but grows it differently. Instead of sorting all edges and worrying about cycles, it keeps one connected tree and repeatedly reaches out to the nearest new vertex. Because the tree is always connected, a new edge can only create a cycle if it joins two vertices already inside — and Prim's never picks such an edge, so no separate cycle check is needed. This makes Prim's especially natural when the graph is given as a distance matrix.

items: **Step 1:** choose any starting vertex; it is the initial tree.
**Step 2:** find the cheapest edge from a vertex in the tree to a vertex not yet in the tree, and add that edge and vertex.
**Step 3:** repeat Step 2 until all $v$ vertices are in the tree.
**No cycle check needed:** you only ever connect a new (outside) vertex, so a cycle can never form.

sections.question: Using the same graph (edges AB 4, AC 3, BC 2, BD 5, CD 6, DE 4, CE 7), apply Prim's algorithm starting from vertex A to find the minimum spanning tree and confirm its weight. [5]
sections.solution:
Begin with the tree containing only A.

**Step 1: from {A}.** Edges leaving A: AC (3), AB (4). Cheapest is AC (3). Add C. Tree = {A, C}. [M1: choose cheapest edge to a new vertex]

**Step 2: from {A, C}.** Edges to outside vertices: AB (4), BC (2), CD (6), CE (7). Cheapest is BC (2). Add B. Tree = {A, B, C}. [A1]

**Step 3: from {A, B, C}.** Edges to outside vertices: BD (5), CD (6), CE (7). Cheapest is BD (5). Add D. Tree = {A, B, C, D}. [A1]

**Step 4: from {A, B, C, D}.** Edges to the last vertex E: DE (4), CE (7). Cheapest is DE (4). Add E. Tree = {A, B, C, D, E}. [M1: continue until all vertices joined]

All five vertices are connected using edges AC, BC, BD, DE.

**Total weight:** $3 + 2 + 5 + 4 = 14.$ [A1]

This is the same tree and the same total weight, 14, that Kruskal's produced — a useful check that both algorithms agree.

sections.content: If a question hands you a distance matrix rather than a diagram, Prim's is usually faster: cross out the starting vertex's row, then repeatedly circle the smallest available entry in the columns of vertices already reached, deleting that vertex's row as you go. Kruskal's, by contrast, shines when the edges are few and easy to list in order. Either is fully acceptable unless the question names one.
The Chinese postman (route inspection) problem

The Chinese postman problem — also called route inspection — asks for the shortest closed walk that travels along every edge of a graph at least once and returns to the start. This is the problem of a postal worker, a gritting lorry, a street cleaner or a road inspector. Whether any edges must be repeated depends on the degrees of the vertices.

items: **Goal:** shortest closed route covering every edge at least once.
**If every vertex has even degree** the graph has an Eulerian circuit, so no edge need be repeated: the route length is just the sum of all edge weights.
**If some vertices have odd degree** (always an even number of them) some edges must be repeated. To find the least extra distance:
1. List the odd-degree vertices.
2. Consider every way to pair them up; for each pairing add the shortest-path distances between the paired vertices.
3. Take the pairing with the smallest total.
4. **Route length = (sum of all edge weights) + (that minimum pairing total).**

sections.question: A groundskeeper must walk along every path in a park and return to the gate. The paths and their lengths (in hundreds of metres) are AB 8, AC 5, BC 4, BD 7, CD 9, CE 6, DE 3. Find the length of the shortest inspection route. [6]
sections.solution:
**Step 1: total of all edge weights.**
$8 + 5 + 4 + 7 + 9 + 6 + 3 = 42.$ [A1]

**Step 2: find the degree of each vertex.**
- deg(A) = 2 (edges AB, AC) — even
- deg(B) = 3 (edges AB, BC, BD) — odd
- deg(C) = 4 (edges AC, BC, CD, CE) — even
- deg(D) = 3 (edges BD, CD, DE) — odd
- deg(E) = 2 (edges CE, DE) — even
The odd-degree vertices are B and D. [M1]

**Step 3: shortest path between the odd pair B and D.**
- Direct BD: $7$.
- B–C–D: $4 + 9 = 13$.
- B–C–E–D: $4 + 6 + 3 = 13$.
The shortest B-to-D distance is the direct edge, $7$. [M1]
With only two odd vertices there is a single pairing, so this $7$ is the least extra distance. [A1]

**Step 4: route length.**
$	ext{route} = 42 + 7 = 49.$ [M1]

**Answer:** the shortest inspection route is $49 	imes 100 = 4900$ metres. [A1]

sections.content: When four vertices are odd the only extra work is the pairing step: with odd vertices P, Q, R, S you compare the three pairings (PQ + RS), (PR + QS) and (PS + QR), using shortest-path distances, and keep the smallest total. Everything else is identical.

The classic slip is to confuse the two route problems. The Chinese postman covers every **edge** (like a postman on paths); the travelling salesman visits every **vertex** (like a salesman calling at places). Read the question for 'every road / street / path' versus 'every town / customer / site' before you choose a method.
The travelling salesman problem (TSP)

The travelling salesman problem asks for the shortest closed tour that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the start — a minimum-weight Hamiltonian cycle. Unlike the spanning tree and the postman, there is no efficient method guaranteed to find the exact optimum: the number of tours grows factorially, so for anything but tiny graphs we settle for trapping the answer. We find an upper bound (a real tour, so the optimum is no worse) and a lower bound (a value the optimum cannot fall below). At this level the graph is usually a complete graph given by a distance table.

items: **Goal:** shortest tour visiting every vertex once and returning to the start.
**Upper bound — nearest-neighbour algorithm:**
1. Start at a chosen vertex.
2. Travel to the nearest unvisited vertex.
3. Repeat until all vertices are visited.
4. Return directly to the start. The total is an upper bound.
**Lower bound — deleted-vertex method:**
1. Delete a chosen vertex and all its edges.
2. Find the MST of the remaining graph.
3. Add the two shortest edges that were attached to the deleted vertex.
4. The sum is a lower bound.
**Conclusion:** the optimal tour length $T$ satisfies lower bound $\le T \le$ upper bound.

sections.question:
A courier starts at depot A, must visit branches B, C, D and E once each, and return to A. The table gives the shortest travel times (minutes) between them.

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | – | 12 | 20 | 15 | 25 |
| B | 12 | – | 10 | 18 | 14 |
| C | 20 | 10 | – | 11 | 16 |
| D | 15 | 18 | 11 | – | 13 |
| E | 25 | 14 | 16 | 13 | – |

(a) Use the nearest-neighbour algorithm from A to find an upper bound. 
(b) By deleting vertex A, find a lower bound. [8]
sections.solution:
**(a) Upper bound — nearest neighbour from A.**
- At A, nearest is B (12). Go to B. [M1: start and move to nearest]
- At B, unvisited nearest is C (10). Go to C.
- At C, unvisited nearest is D (11). Go to D.
- At D, only E is left (13). Go to E.
- Return E → A (25) to close the tour. [M1: complete the tour back to A]
Tour: A → B → C → D → E → A.
Length $= 12 + 10 + 11 + 13 + 25 = 71$ minutes. So an upper bound is $71$. [A1]

**(b) Lower bound — delete A.**
Remove A and its edges, leaving vertices B, C, D, E. [M1]
Find the MST of {B, C, D, E}. Edges in order: BC (10), CD (11), DE (13), BE (14), CE (16), BD (18).
- Accept BC (10).
- Accept CD (11).
- Accept DE (13). That is $v - 1 = 3$ edges connecting all four vertices; stop.
MST weight $= 10 + 11 + 13 = 34$. [A1]
Add the two shortest edges from the deleted vertex A: from AB (12), AC (20), AD (15), AE (25) the two smallest are AB (12) and AD (15). [M1]
Lower bound $= 34 + 12 + 15 = 61$ minutes. [A1]

**Conclusion:** the optimal tour time $T$ satisfies $61 \le T \le 71$ minutes.

sections.content: Two honest caveats. The nearest-neighbour tour is rarely optimal — running it from a different start vertex can give a smaller (better) upper bound, and you keep the smallest. Likewise, deleting a different vertex can give a larger (better) lower bound, and you keep the largest. Tightening both bounds narrows the interval that must contain the true optimum.
Choosing the right algorithm

Half the marks in this topic are won by classifying the problem correctly before you compute. A spanning tree joins every vertex with no loops and is about the cheapest connection. The Chinese postman covers every edge and returns home. The travelling salesman visits every vertex once and returns home. Confusing the postman and the salesman is the most common and most expensive error, because the whole method changes.

items: **Connect all points, least total, no loops** → minimum spanning tree (Kruskal's or Prim's).
**Cover every road / edge, return to start** → Chinese postman (pair odd-degree vertices).
**Visit every place / vertex once, return to start** → travelling salesman (nearest-neighbour upper bound, deleted-vertex lower bound).
**Spanning tree uses $v-1$ edges; a tour uses $v$ edges** (it must close the loop).

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Adding one edge too many (or too few) to an MST** — a spanning tree on $v$ vertices has exactly $v-1$ edges and no cycle. Adding a $v$-th edge always closes a loop; stopping early leaves a vertex disconnected.

**Skipping the cycle check in Kruskal's** — you must reject the cheapest edge if it closes a loop, and show that rejection. Blindly taking the $v-1$ cheapest edges can give a graph with a cycle and a missing vertex.
**Confusing the postman with the salesman** — the postman covers every EDGE, the salesman visits every VERTEX. Choosing the wrong one loses the whole question.

**Pairing odd vertices with direct edges instead of shortest paths** — in the Chinese postman you repeat the shortest PATH between an odd pair, which may run through other vertices, not necessarily the single connecting edge.
**Treating the nearest-neighbour tour as the exact answer** — it is only an upper bound; it is a valid tour but usually not the shortest.
**Adding the two longest deleted edges for the lower bound** — you add the two SHORTEST edges at the deleted vertex to the MST of the rest; using the longest destroys the bound.

**Forgetting to return to the start** — both the postman route and the salesman tour are closed: the salesman tour therefore has $v$ edges, not $v-1$. Omitting the final edge back to the start undercounts.
**Reporting the wrong direction of the interval** — the optimal TSP length lies between the lower and upper bounds; quote it as lower $\le T \le$ upper, not the reverse.

sections.content: Where this leads

These three algorithms are your introduction to discrete optimisation, the branch of mathematics behind route planning, network design and scheduling. The greedy idea that powers Kruskal's and nearest neighbour reappears whenever a problem is solved one locally best step at a time; the bounding idea behind the TSP — trapping an answer you cannot compute exactly between something achievable and something unbeatable — is a habit of mind used throughout applied mathematics and computer science. Master the classification first, then the mechanics, and always show the ordered working: the method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when a single weight slips.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 network question marked: build the MST, pair the odd vertices, or bound the tour with full working
